text: The video shows the conventional positioning of the surgical port used for radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to expose the right side, aiming to locate the bladder vessels.
The surgeon dissects around the bladder vessels, focusing on the umbilical artery, which is visible and clipped during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the bladder laterally, carefully preserving the nerve that supplies the penis and pelvic muscles, until reaching the prostate. The dissection aims to isolate the structures at the base of the prostate, similar to techniques used in radical prostatectomy.

The surgeon avoids thermic coagulation while pushing the visceral part of the endopelvic fascia, revealing the vesicle prostatic vessels at the base of the prostate. The vessels are cut at the level of the bladder neck.

The surgeon identifies the prostate and the anatomical boundary between the bladder and prostate. The right neurovascular bundle is dissected and appears completely free. The surgeon then transitions to dissecting the ureter, located just behind the umbilical artery.
The surgeon clips the urethra and opens the Douglas pouch, while preserving the vascularization. The previously dissected vessels of the prostate are visible.

The surgeon cuts the superior vesicular artery, controlling the residual vessel. The bladder and prostate are then moved completely to the left side. The Deneuve-Villiers fascia is pushed aside, revealing the surgical field. The patient, a 58-year-old with carcinoma in situ on the superior part of the bladder, has had the right side dissected, and the surgeon now moves to the left.
The surgeon isolates the umbilical artery and the superior vesicle artery using cold cutting techniques, avoiding thermic coagulation to preserve functional outcomes.
The surgeon removes fat tissue from the bladder, performing an anatomical dissection. The Douglas pouch is opened completely, revealing the vas deferens and the left ureter, which is then controlled.

The surgeon cuts the ureter, performing a limited dissection to preserve its vascularization. A small clip is applied at the origin of the ureter. The bladder is shown dissected posteriorly, with one or two small branches visible on the left. A vein from the bladder and prostate is seen extending to the dorsal vein complex, where a 10 or 5 millimeter clip is used.

The surgeon demonstrates the complete posterior dissection of the bladder, preparing to cut the left vas deferens. The endopelvic fascia is visible on the left side of the prostate, with distinct bundles observed posteriorly. The surgical approach progresses from the right, then posteriorly, and finally to the left.

The surgeon focuses on the posterior aspect of the prostate, carefully preserving the Denonvilliers fascia. The bladder has been completely dissected posteriorly, and the surgeon proceeds with anterior dissection to ensure a clear operating field.

The surgeon identifies the prostate, bladder, and dorsal vein complex, preparing to cut the dorsal vein complex while preserving the anterior fixation of the urethra. The muscles extending from the bladder neck to the pubis bone, known as the aprons, are visible. The fascia is pushed close to the apex to preserve structures important for early continence.

The surgeon demonstrates the bladder and the dorsal vein complex, noting that stitches are not necessary for control due to the clear vision of the apex. The apex of the prostate is dissected laterally and posteriorly, with the laparoscopic gas pressure at twelve effectively stopping any bleeding from the dorsal vein complex.

The surgeon pushes the endopelvic fascia laterally to reach the apex of the prostate, revealing the urethra after removing the catheter. There is minor bleeding observed around the urethra, which is not significant at this stage.

The surgeon cuts the urethra at the level of the verru montanum, completing the cystectomy. The assistant places a name block at the apex of the prostate. The surgical field shows the prepared urethra, with occasional need for stitches.

The surgical field shows the left ureter and umbilical artery, with the preserved anterior wall of the vagina and uterus visible. The left ureter is seen with a double G stent due to previous tumor resections. The surgeon moves to the right side, revealing the right ureter.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and ureter as key anatomical landmarks, performing lateral dissection of the bladder. The umbilical artery and superior vesicle artery are visible on the right side, and cold dissection is used to preserve vascularization and achieve a good functional result.

The surgeon begins dissection between the bladder and uterus, identifying the round ligament on the left side. The iliac artery and umbilical artery are visible, with the superior vesicle artery being clipped for clear dissection of the bladder.

The surgeon dissects between the bladder and uterus, advancing the dissection towards the trigone. A metallic valve is placed in the vagina for manipulation and clear visualization. The medial dissection under the bladder is performed while preserving the nerve structures leading to the bladder neck and maintaining the integrity of the lateral wall of the vagina.

The surgeon is dissecting under the bladder neck, identifying the inferior bladder artery. The right side is more challenging to dissect due to extensive resections from previous treatments for bladder tumor.

The surgeon carefully preserves the first part of the urethra, along with the vascularization and innervation of the bladder neck, by dissecting millimeter by millimeter using clips. The lateral face of the bladder is dissected without thermic regulation, similar to the technique used in male patients.

The surgeon is working on the anterior aspect of the surgical field, clipping vessels laterally under the bladder neck. The vagina is visible under the balloon, and the surgeon cuts at the junction between the bladder and urethra, with the dorsal vein complex and uterus also visible.

The surgeon inflates the balloon to five cc for a cold dissection at the bladder neck and first part of the urethra. A clip is placed at the bladder neck, and the urethra is cut while protecting surrounding nerves and muscles. The surgeon performs a left node dissection, opens the anterior wall of the vagina, and removes the bladder and nodes. The vagina is closed distally to prevent fistulas, and the urethra is visible for neobladder reconstruction.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and positions it behind the artery following the left node dissection. A small incision is made around one centimeter, and the ileal loop is sutured to the urethra using a rolling suture technique. The ileal loop is cut to a length of twenty-five to thirty centimeters on both sides, and a lateral-lateral anastomosis is performed using a stapler.

The surgeon opens the neobladder on the anti-mesenteric area, creating a V-shaped incision. Using a ProGrasp instrument, the posterior part of the bladder is closed with a V-Loc 3-0 suture, applying tension every two passages. The extremity of the posterior part is sutured, preferring to take the musculosa layer. An anterior reconstruction of the urethra is performed over two centimeters, resulting in a neobladder with two branches.

The surgeon uses the Prograsp instrument to hold the left part of the bladder, closing it with a 26-gauge needle. The bladder is shaped like a heart, with the superior part of the posterior layer coming anteriorly. The ureters are implanted in front of the neobladder without crossing the urethra, minimizing dissection. The morphology of the bladder is observed, and the water tightness is tested.

The surgeon removes the ureter from behind the iliac artery and cuts it. The neobladder is opened, and a triangular needle with 5-0 monocryl is used for stitching without tension. The posterior and anterior layers are visible, and the ureter is positioned without stents, aiming to reduce infection risk. The final neobladder structure is shown, similar to that in women, with two ureters.

The surgeon demonstrates the ureteric implantation into the neobladder, performing between eight and ten suture passages. The quality of the stitches is tested multiple times. The bladder is opened, and the procedure is conducted laparoscopically with a triangular needle to minimize tension and traction, using a two-needle driver for precision.

The surgeon uses a double G stent to ensure the ureteric implantation is watertight, testing for leaks at the anastomosis sites. The final position of the neobladder is shown, highlighting the importance of preserving ureter vascularization during dissection.
